Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a fixed `pkgver` and a single source entry downloading an AppImage from the project's own GitHub releases over HTTPS. The `sha256sums` array is pinned to a specific hash, not `SKIP`, so integrity verification will occur. No code is executed in global scope or `pkgver()` that could perform malicious actions—only variable assignments and a function definition (`package()`) that is never invoked during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. Fetching the source via HTTPS and checking its SHA-256 sum is standard and safe. No dangerous commands (eval, curl|bash, exfiltration, etc.) are present in the evaluated scope.
</details>
<summary>Safe source fetch with pinned checksum.</summary>
</security_assessment>

file_verdicts.summary: Standard AppImage packaging; no malicious activity detected.

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/wispr-flow-appimage.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-qnb2jmy6...

Analyzing wispr-flow-appimage AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-qnb2jmy6) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source download from a GitHub release URL using a hardcoded AppImage filename and a corresponding SHA-256 checksum. The `source` array entry uses a fixed tag derived from `pkgver`, which is statically defined and does not involve command substitution or dynamic fetching. The `pkgver()` function is absent, so no dynamic version calculation occurs during sourcing. There is no code executed at the top level or within `pkgver()` that would perform network requests, file operations, or system modifications when the PKGBUILD is sourced. The use of `::` in the source line is valid syntax for renaming downloaded files and does not introduce risk. All operations are limited to declaring sources and metadata, consistent with safe AUR packaging practices.

The two commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only parse the PKGBUILD, download the specified AppImage (as declared in `source`), and verify its checksum. No build or prepare functions are run, and no untrusted code is executed during this phase. The AppImage itself is not extracted or run at this stage, even though `noextract` is set. The remaining functions like `package()` are irrelevant for this safety gate, as they are not executed during the specified commands.

Standard AUR practices are followed: the source is publicly verifiable, the checksum is pinned, and no VCS sources or SKIP checksums are used. There is no evidence of obfuscated code, data exfiltration, or remote code execution during the sourcing or source-fetching phase.
</details>
<summary>
No execution risk during source fetch and parse
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No execution risk during source fetch and parse[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches a prebuilt AppImage from the project's official GitHub release page using a deterministic versioned URL and includes a fixed SHA-256 checksum for integrity verification. The package does not build from source but instead extracts the AppImage contents to install them system-wide, avoiding runtime FUSE dependency. This is a common and acceptable practice for AppImage packaging in AUR.

The installation process is transparent and limited to expected operations: setting execute permissions, extracting the image, copying files to standard system directories (`/opt`, `/usr/bin`, `/usr/share`), and installing a desktop entry and icon. It does not perform any network requests during `package()`, uses no obfuscated code, and avoids dangerous runtime injection (e.g., `eval`, `curl|bash`). All actions are scoped to packaging the upstream-provided binary without modification.

The source is pinned to a specific release tag and file, with checksum provided, reducing supply-chain risk. While the package distributes a binary blob (inherent to AppImage), this aligns with the package's description and does not constitute a security issue in the AUR packaging context.
</details>
<summary>
Packages a verified AppImage safely</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Packages a verified AppImage safely
Reviewed all required files.
Deciding which files to review next...
Decided to review 1 additional files: PKGBUILD.template
Reviewing additional files...
Reviewing additional files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD.template...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD.template:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD.template is a standard packaging script for an AUR package that wraps a prebuilt AppImage of the Wispr Flow application. It downloads the AppImage from the project's official GitHub releases URL, verifies its integrity via a placeholder for a SHA256 checksum (to be filled at release time), and extracts it locally to install the application under `/opt` and create appropriate symlinks and desktop integration.

The operations performed are consistent with AppImage packaging best practices: `--appimage-extract` is used instead of relying on FUSE at runtime, icons and desktop entries are extracted and properly rewritten, and file permissions are correctly set. There are no network calls at runtime, no use of `eval`, `base64`, or obfuscated code, and no attempts to exfiltrate data or modify system files outside the package directory. The source URL points to the same GitHub repository declared in the `url` field, and checksums are intended to be provided (though templated). The use of a template with placeholders (`%%PKGVER%%`, etc.) is a common practice in automated AUR publishing workflows and does not introduce risk when properly managed.

While the AppImage itself is not built from source and is proprietary (declared via `LicenseRef-proprietary`), this is disclosed in the `pkgdesc` and is not a supply-chain issue with the PKGBUILD itself. The package depends only on common system libraries, and optional clipboard support for Wayland is logically scoped. No unsafe or unexpected behavior is present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AppImage packaging; no malicious activity detected.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD.template. Status: SAFE -- Standard AppImage packaging; no malicious activity detected.

data.reasoning: Both models correctly identified the PKGBUILD and template use pinned checksums, HTTPS sourcing from GitHub, and standard AppImage installation procedures with no dangerous commands or hidden behaviors.
coverage_issues: Both models skipped binary AppImage analysis - inherent tool limitation
No review of actual extracted AppImage contents (e.g., runtime scripts)
false_negatives: Potential risks in the skipped AppImage binary (e.g., embedded scripts with network/filesystem access) were not assessed
learnings: Improve binary handling: Flag packages containing binaries as higher-risk and suggest manual review
Prioritize actual install hooks over templates (PKGBUILD > PKGBUILD.template)
Enhance context: When binaries are skipped, explicitly note risks of unaudited payload execution
Add heuristic: Packages with single-binary sources should trigger warnings about unaudited code
